items.title: Latitudinal patterns and environmental correlates of plant root exudation
items.abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03103-4 From an assembled dataset of plant root exudation rate, root traits and environmental covariates, this study shows that the rate of carbon exuded by roots generally increases from high to low latitudes, and soil phosphorus deficiency is the most important influencing factor.

items.title: Pricing nature into sovereign debt
items.abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03101-6 A study that couples ecological–economic scenarios with a machine-learned replica of how nations’ credit ratings are determined shows that even partial losses of just three ecosystem services could materially raise the cost of government borrowing.

items.title: Halting genetic diversity loss, from local to international action and policy
items.abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00162-0 The loss of genetic diversity is a major threat to population resilience, posing a challenge for conservation efforts. This Review highlights how the integration of large-scale biodiversity monitoring datasets and population genetics principles can empower biodiversity partners across governance levels to make effective conservation decisions.
items.oneLine: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00162-0 The loss of genetic diversity is a major threat to population resilience, posing a challenge for conservation efforts. items.title: Different planning scales for renewable expansion yield distinct biodiversity trade-offs in China
items.abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03097-z In this study, China’s solar and wind expansion through to 2060 is projected under national versus provincial planning scenarios, which represent different centralization levels. Modelling shows that each pathway results in distinct trade-offs with biodiversity, which demonstrates the need for integrated, multiscale siting to align climate and biodiversity conservation goals.
items.oneLine: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03097-z In this study, China’s solar and wind expansion through to 2060 is projected under national versus provincial planning scenarios, which represent different centralization levels. items.title: Twentieth-century insect declines and partial recoveries driven by changes in land use and climate
items.abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03083-5 Continuous 90-year trends of butterflies and deadwood-dependent beetles show clear mid-century declines and only partial recovery of these taxa. The declines were clearly related to agricultural intensification, and climate change and more biodiversity-friendly management helped in the recovery and stabilization — although butterflies are still far from their original diversity.
items.oneLine: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03083-5 Continuous 90-year trends of butterflies and deadwood-dependent beetles show clear mid-century declines and only partial recovery of these taxa. items.title: Gene body methylation suppresses intragenic transcription and permits epigenetic inheritance in a cnidarian
items.abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03090-6 Experiments in the cnidarian Nematostella vectensis show a role of gene body methylation in transposable element suppression and that epigenetic inheritance is constrained by chromatin context and transcriptional activity.

items.title: Ninety-year trends reveal sharpest insect declines in the mid-twentieth century
items.abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03074-6 Assessing the magnitude and drivers of recent biodiversity losses requires informative, long-term baseline data. Here the authors reconstruct the temporal trajectories of butterflies and saproxylic beetles in Switzerland from 1930 to 2021, shedding light on the role of climate and land-use change.
items.oneLine: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03074-6 Assessing the magnitude and drivers of recent biodiversity losses requires informative, long-term baseline data. items.abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03087-1 This paper presents sPYce, a method that integrates single-nucleus ATAC-seq data across species, allowing for comparison of gene regulatory evolution across species.

items.title: A hidden pollutant is changing how the world's forests breathe
items.abstract: A massive global analysis found that nitrogen pollution can either speed up or dramatically slow the natural "breathing" of forest soils, depending on the ecosystem's condition. The results reveal hidden tipping points that could affect how forests store carbon and cope with climate change.
items.oneLine: A massive global analysis found that nitrogen pollution can either speed up or dramatically slow the natural "breathing" of forest soils, depending on the ecosystem's condition. The results reveal hidden tipping points that could affect how forests store carbon and cope with climate change.
